chunk_summary: This section discusses zinc deficiency therapy in adolescents, emphasizing proper dosing, dietary strategies, and the risk of copper deficiency with prolonged high-dose zinc supplementation.
learning_objectives: Understand the clinical signs and risk factors for zinc deficiency in adolescents.
Learn appropriate zinc supplementation dosages and therapy duration for subclinical and severe cases.
Recognize the risk of copper deficiency with chronic high-dose zinc use and its management.
Promote food-first strategies to address micronutrient deficiencies in Indian diets.
key_facts: Zinc deficiency therapy for subclinical cases: 1 mg/kg/day of elemental zinc in 1–3 divided doses.
Severe cases like acrodermatitis enteropathica may require 3 mg/kg/day with specialist co-management.
Prolonged high-dose zinc can impair copper absorption, leading to anemia, neutropenia, and neurologic symptoms.
Dietary anchors for zinc include eggs, dairy, dals, legumes, nuts, seeds, and whole grains.
High-phytate diets (e.g., cereal-heavy patterns) can reduce zinc absorption; variety and preparation matter.
reference_quote: Clinical Practice Guidelines: Micronutrient deficiency (RCH) — Food-first anchors in Indian diets include eggs and dairy (if acceptable), dals and legumes (chana, rajma), nuts and seeds, and whole grains.
document_key_topics: Copper deficiency risk with high-dose zinc
Dietary patterns and absorption in Indian contexts
